name: Targeted Individuals
description: The "Targeted Individuals" (TI) phenomenon refers to a belief held by some individuals that they are systematically subjected to surveillance, harassment, and psychological or physical attacks by organized groups, often attributed to government agencies, corporations, or other powerful entities. Proponents claim these attacks can range from electronic harassment (e.g., directed-energy weapons, mind control) and stalking to smear campaigns and professional sabotage. The origins of this belief are complex, potentially stemming from anxieties about government surveillance, technological advancements, and personal experiences of harassment or persecution. Key evidence cited by TIs includes personal testimonies, perceived patterns of unusual events, and interpretations of technological phenomena. Mainstream scholarly perspectives generally attribute these experiences to mental health issues such as delusional disorders, paranoia, and other psychological conditions. Skeptics also point to the lack of verifiable physical evidence supporting the existence of organized targeting programs and the potential for confirmation bias in interpreting ambiguous events. The phenomenon has gained traction online, with numerous websites and forums dedicated to sharing experiences and discussing potential explanations. The lack of a universally accepted definition and the reliance on subjective experiences make it difficult to study objectively.
subtitle: Belief in Organized Surveillance and Harassment

claims.claim: Targeted Individuals are subjected to electronic harassment via directed-energy weapons.
claims.evidence: Personal testimonies of feeling burning sensations, hearing buzzing noises, and experiencing other physical symptoms.
claims.criticism: Mainstream science attributes these symptoms to psychological factors, environmental factors, or undiagnosed medical conditions. There is no scientific evidence to support the existence of directed-energy weapons being used in this way.

claims.claim: Government agencies or other powerful entities are conducting covert surveillance and harassment campaigns against TIs.
claims.evidence: Perceived patterns of suspicious activity, such as constant surveillance, stalking, and smear campaigns.
claims.criticism: These perceptions can be explained by psychological factors such as paranoia, anxiety, and confirmation bias. Legitimate surveillance is subject to legal oversight and requires warrants.

claims.claim: Mind control technologies are being used to manipulate the thoughts and behaviors of TIs.
claims.evidence: Subjective experiences of thought insertion, thought broadcasting, and other forms of mental interference.
claims.criticism: Mainstream science considers mind control technologies to be pseudoscience. These experiences are often attributed to mental health conditions and cognitive biases.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Katherine Horton
keyFigures.role: Advocate for TIs and researcher of alleged electronic harassment.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Steven Hassan
keyFigures.role: Expert on cults and mind control, sometimes cited by TIs to explain their experiences.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Lorraine Sheridan
keyFigures.role: Psychologist specializing in stalking and harassment, whose research provides alternative explanations for TI experiences.

mythologyConnections: Demonic possession (as a metaphor for feeling controlled)
Curses and hexes (paralleling the feeling of being targeted)

timeline.event: Emergence of concerns about government surveillance and technological control.
Development of online communities for individuals claiming to be Targeted Individuals.
Continued growth of the TI movement and ongoing debate about the validity of their claims.

sources.author: Karen Douglas, Robbie Sutton, Aleksandra Cichocka
sources.title: Karen Douglas, Robbie Sutton, Aleksandra Cichocka

sources.author: David Voas
sources.work: The Rise and Fall of the New Age
sources.title: David Voas
